narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—about a 66% chance of not passing—leading to this hard decision on your application. Picture the model as an experienced advisor looking at patterns from thousands of students like you, and what stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 10, the top of your class academically that year, but in a way that signaled challenges ahead rather than a strong launchpad for the bar. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 10, another high mark early on, yet together these painted a picture of inconsistent momentum that often trips up even dedicated students in the long haul toward passing. Your upper-middle family income added to that narrative, as the model has seen how it sometimes correlates with added pressures that pull focus away from bar prep. Even with your solid LSAT score of 45, which is respectable, it didn't quite counterbalance those trends in the model's eyes. Your white background and undergraduate GPA of 3.4 played supporting roles, each nudging the outlook toward struggle based on historical patterns we've observed. Your gender as female and choice to go full-time were noted too, with full-time actually being a small bright spot tilting slightly toward success. It's this combination—strong but uneven school deciles at the forefront, layered with income and scores—that convinced the model your overall profile matches too many who've faltered on the bar despite early promise. This isn't about doubting your potential; it's the model highlighting risks from similar stories to help schools build stronger classes. You have real strengths here, like those top deciles and LSAT, that could shine with targeted bar prep or a different path. Keep pushing—you're closer than this one prediction suggests.
